frameworks.name: LTV-Conversion Rate Model Selection Test
frameworks.definition: Compare two business models by asking three questions: (1) Does more of the target market want it (conversion rate)? (2) Is the lifetime gross profit per customer higher? (3) Is the CAC comparable or lower? The model that wins on all three is mathematically superior.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that pursuing two business models simultaneously decreases the probability of achieving either goal, because founder attention and execution capacity are finite and split focus compounds errors in both models rather than compounding progress in one.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: doing both will decrease the likelihood that you hit either
conditions: founder is simultaneously scaling an existing profitable service business
founder is also building a new licensing or adjacent offer
claims.mechanism: Founder attention and execution capacity are finite; split focus compounds errors in both models rather than compounding progress in one.
bounded_by: If a founder chooses all-in on the new model and fully stops growing the old one, the argument does not apply

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a new, untested business model appears attractive primarily because its problems are invisible to the operator, whereas the existing model's problems are highly visible after years of operation — creating a false comparison that systematically biases founders toward abandoning proven businesses.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you just don't know the problems that exist with it yet because you haven't done enough of it
conditions: operator has run existing model long enough to know its problems intimately
operator has only run the new model for a short period (insufficient to surface its problems)
claims.mechanism: The new model's costs and failure modes have not yet materialized; the comparison is therefore between the known interior of the old model and the attractive exterior of the new model — a structurally biased comparison.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the operator has run both models at scale for comparable durations

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a done-for-you service business has 40-50% margins, 12-month client agreements, and clients staying past 12 months, it constitutes a highly valuable core business that should be protected and grown rather than abandoned in favor of a licensing or training offer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service business operating at 40-50% gross margin
client contracts structured as 12-month agreements
majority of clients renewing past 12 months

numbers.axis: gross margin on done-for-you service business

anchors.region: threshold Hormozi calls a golden goose

bounded_by: If the business model is genuinely locally constrained and cannot be scaled nationally, the calculus changes (though Hormozi notes the podcast agency is not locally constrained)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when comparing two business models, the mathematically correct choice is the one with the higher conversion rate (more of the market wants it) AND the higher lifetime gross profit per customer — and that this is a math question, not a feeling question.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: we have to solve for maximum LTV and conversion rate
conditions: operator is deciding between two viable business models
sufficient data exists to estimate conversion rate and LTV for each model
bounded_by: Does not apply when only one model has sufficient data to estimate LTV

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends a model transition sequence in which the operator continues selling the new superior model while keeping existing clients of the old model, then stops replacing old-model clients as they churn, until the new model's revenue and profit exceeds the old model's total — at which point the old model is wound down passively.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: keep selling this on the front end until eventually this surpasses the total revenue and profit of the other business
conditions: new model has been validated as superior on LTV and conversion rate
old model has sticky long-term clients who should not be abandoned abruptly

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a done-for-you service business constrained by team capacity should resolve the constraint by hiring more team members, not by switching to a licensing model — because the licensing pivot is a response to operational pain rather than a genuine strategic improvement.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: then you just hire more people to the team
conditions: business has proven margins and sticky clients
team is overwhelmed but the business model itself is sound
bounded_by: If the business model itself is fundamentally unscalable (e.g., locally constrained), the hire-more answer may not be sufficient

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that when a done-for-you client business with strong retention exists alongside a licensing offer that serves those same clients' own distribution needs, the operator can refer done-for-you clients into the licensing base and take a spread — achieving 100% margins on that referral layer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: just take a spread. And then you have 100% margins on that.
conditions: operator has done-for-you clients who could also benefit from the operator's systems
operator licenses systems to those same clients for their own use

numbers.axis: gross margin on referral/spread layer when selling licensing to existing done-for-you clients

anchors.region: spread from placing done-for-you clients into licensing base

claims.text: Alex Hormozi notes that his own decision to pursue a licensing/franchise model with Gym Launch rather than owning gyms directly was driven by local geographic constraint — and that he is uncertain it was the best decision in retrospect, suggesting operators with nationally scalable businesses should not use Gym Launch as the template for pivoting away from direct service delivery.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: I don't necessarily know if the decision I made was the best decision. It was just the decision I made.
conditions: operator's existing done-for-you business is not geographically constrained
operator is considering licensing as path to scale
bounded_by: Applies specifically when the original business model has no geographic ceiling; does not apply to brick-and-mortar or location-dependent businesses where licensing is the natural scaling path

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a service business with 12-month client agreements and the majority of clients renewing past 12 months has superior retention economics, and that growing this type of business by acquiring more clients is the highest-value use of founder attention.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you just have a golden goose that you have 12 months stick
conditions: clients are on 12-month agreements
clients are renewing past the 12-month term
business has 40%+ gross margins

numbers.axis: client contract length and renewal behavior

anchors.region: Vibes Creative podcast agency client cohort
threshold for Hormozi's golden goose label

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a licensing offer designed to help end-clients run their own podcast (by placing staff and providing systems) is fundamentally different from — and more viable than — a licensing offer designed to help clients start podcast agencies, because the former targets the much larger market of people who want a podcast, whereas the latter targets the small market of people who want to build an agency.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: This makes me feel significantly better. I'm actually not opposed to it.
conditions: licensing offer is targeting end-clients who want the outcome (a podcast), not people who want to replicate the agency business

examples.text: Vibes Creative, an Australian video podcast agency, built a done-for-you podcasting service at $10,000 per month charging seven-to-nine-figure entrepreneurs, achieving 40-50% gross margins and 12-month client agreements with most clients renewing past 12 months. The founder came to Alex Hormozi considering a pivot to an $11,000 licensing offer that would teach clients to run their own podcasts by placing a video editor and social media manager for them, partly because the team was overwhelmed and partly because he believed licensing could scale to $1 million per month faster. Hormozi challenged the premise: with 40-50% margins and 12-month sticky clients, the done-for-you business was already a golden goose, and the licensing pivot was a distraction driven by operational pain and shiny-object syndrome, not a genuine strategic improvement. After further questioning revealed the licensing offer served end-clients (people who want a podcast) rather than aspiring agency owners, Hormozi endorsed the licensing model as viable — but still recommended a passive wind-down of the done-for-you side rather than a hard pivot.

Alex Hormozi used a relationship analogy to illustrate hidden cost blindness: a founder comparing their current business model to a new one is like a person comparing a long-term partner (whose flaws are fully known) to a new person who has just walked into their life (whose flaws are entirely hidden). The new model looks better because you know the old model's problems intimately but have not yet discovered the new model's equivalent problems — the crazy ex-boyfriend, the personality issues. The decision is always made before the new model's interior is visible.

practices.text: When your done-for-you service team is overwhelmed and capacity-constrained, hire more people rather than pivoting to a licensing or training model.
Before comparing two business models, convert the comparison to a math question: which model has a higher conversion rate (more people want it) and a higher lifetime gross profit per customer?

When transitioning from an old business model to a new one, do not abruptly shut down the old model; instead keep selling the new model until its revenue and profit exceeds the old model, then stop replacing old-model clients as they naturally churn.

If you operate a done-for-you service and also have a licensing offer serving the same client avatar, refer done-for-you clients into the licensing base and take a margin spread with zero incremental cost.
